summary: DHH describes the universal frustration of forgetting to recharge devices overnight. Despite having the intention to plug in gadgets like laptops, iPods, and cameras before bed, the thought evaporates the moment he walks through the door. He proposes a comedic solution inspired by the film Memento: chanting 'gotta recharge' like someone unable to form long-term memories. The post is a lighthearted early blog entry about the friction of living with battery-dependent technology.
key_points: Rechargeable batteries create a deceptively difficult memory challenge in daily life
The intention to recharge evaporates between forming the thought and acting on it
Repetitive verbal chanting is proposed as a hack to bridge short-term memory gaps
The post references Memento as a metaphor for how fleeting mundane intentions can be
money_quotes: Rechargeable batteries are everywhere, and they require a decievingly hard skill to remain effective: keeping short term memory from evaporating.

Just before I enter my apartment, I'll have the need to plugin said gadget into a power outlet in mind. And in the morning, I'll find myself swearing, when the iPod still cries for a recharge, just as I'm about to leave.
The only way to remember the trite of recharging is to act like you're suffering from the inability to make long-term memories.
Chant the following all the way up the stairs or riding the elevator, 'Gotta recharge, gotta recharge, gotta recharge'.
key_insight: Even the simplest daily tasks like recharging devices expose how fragile our short-term intentions are once we cross a threshold into a new context.
